executive_summary: February's survey paints a picture of rising nervousness among professional money managers even as everyday wealthy investors keep faith in the market's ability to bounce back. Institutional one-year optimism dropped sharply, and crash worry among the pros spiked to its highest level in our three-month window. Yet individual investors remain remarkably willing to buy the dip — 80% expect a quick recovery after a big sell-off. The two groups are reading the same market and arriving at notably different conclusions.

key_findings.headline: Optimism Fades Across the Board
key_findings.interpretation: Both groups pulled back on their 12-month expectations — institutions dropped roughly 27 points from January, while individuals slipped about 9 points. For the first time in this window, the pros are barely more optimistic than everyday investors, which may suggest institutional confidence is eroding faster than the broader mood.

key_findings.headline: Pros and Public Diverge Sharply
key_findings.interpretation: Institutional crash confidence jumped dramatically — from 23% to 58% — meaning fewer pros now worry about an imminent crash. Individual crash confidence also rose modestly. The wide gap between the two groups could reflect different interpretations of the same economic signals, or it may simply show that professionals recalibrated after a jittery January.

key_findings.headline: Dip-Buying Faith Holds Strong
key_findings.interpretation: Both groups grew more confident that a sharp one-day decline would bounce back within 48 hours. Individual investors lead the charge at 80%, up from 73% in January. This resilient buy-the-dip mentality could reflect trust in market liquidity — or it may hint at a complacency that hasn't been tested by a real downturn recently.

key_findings.headline: Valuation Views Flip Dramatically
key_findings.interpretation: Only a third of institutional investors feel the market is fairly priced, down from 58% in January — a 25-point swing toward seeing overvaluation. Meanwhile, individual confidence in valuations surged from just 7% to 52%. It's worth noting that some institutional investors subscribe to market-efficiency views, where 'overvaluation' is essentially a meaningless concept since prices always reflect available information.

detailed_analysis:
The headline story in February is a growing split between how professional money managers and wealthy individual investors feel about risk. Institutional one-year confidence fell to roughly 55% — down from 82% just a month earlier — while individual investors only dipped modestly. When the people who manage money for a living get more cautious while the people who own that money stay upbeat, it's a tension worth watching, though it doesn't necessarily tell us who's right.

Crash confidence tells an interesting and somewhat counterintuitive story. The pros actually became less worried about an imminent crash in February, even as their longer-term optimism faded. One reading of this: institutional investors may believe that near-term catalysts for a sudden collapse have receded, even if the 12-month picture looks murkier. It's a bit like feeling confident your roof won't leak today while still worrying about the forecast next week.

Buy-on-dips confidence continued its upward march in both groups, with individual investors now at 80%. This could reflect genuine faith in market resilience — or it could be a habit formed during years of quick recoveries that may not always hold. Either way, both groups increasingly treat sharp sell-offs as buying opportunities rather than warning signs.

The wildest swing belongs to individual valuation confidence, which rocketed from a dismal 7% in January to 52% in February. That January reading was unusually low and may have been influenced by small sample variation or a temporary mood swing. The February rebound brings individuals closer to historical norms. Institutional investors, meanwhile, moved in the opposite direction — only a third now see the market as fairly valued, the lowest in our three-month window. For those who hold efficient-market views, this debate is somewhat academic: if prices reflect all known information, the question of overvaluation doesn't really apply.
market_context: February's readings came as the early weeks of the U.S.-Iran conflict were pushing Brent crude toward $120 a barrel and dragging equity markets to multi-month lows. Since then, a two-week ceasefire announced on April 7 triggered one of the market's best single days in over a year — oil fell more than 15% and the S&P 500 jumped 2.5% — offering some relief after a prolonged stretch of losses. The ceasefire is tentative, tariff tensions with major trading partners remain unsettled, and markets have made clear they'll react sharply to any headline that changes either picture.
methodology: These indices come from the Yale/Shiller U.S. Stock Market Confidence Surveys, which have tracked investor sentiment since 1989. Each month, roughly 100 wealthy individual investors and 100 institutional investment managers answer the same standardized questions. The consistency of those questions over 35+ years is what makes the data meaningful — you can actually compare investor psychology across very different market environments. The four indices each measure something distinct: • 1-Year Confidence: The share who expect prices to be higher in 12 months. • Crash Confidence: The share who believe a crash in the next 6 months is very unlikely (under 10% probability). A low reading means most people think a crash is plausible. • Buy-on-Dips Confidence: The share who believe that after a 3% single-day market drop, prices would fully recover within two days. • Valuation Confidence: The share who do not believe the market is overvalued. Surveys were conducted semi-annually before 2001 and have been monthly since July 2001. Respondents rotate over time, but consistent characteristics — high income, investment experience — are maintained across the panel.
